title: L03.1.list tuples
body:
Toolkit for Finance and Financial Decision Making Lecture 3.1: Data structures, lists, and tuples

Breno Schmidt and Robert Tumarkin UNSW

Overview

Overview of data structures  lists, tuples, sets, dictionaries Lists  Properties and construction  Indexing  Commonly used list methods Tuples  Properties and construction  Tuples vs lists

Data Structures  Data Structures

Python provides a number of containers in its standard library, called data structures

Data structures are collections of Python objects We will focus on four commonly used data structures:

 list: ordered and mutable (changeable) collections  tuple: ordered and immutable (unchangeable) collections  set: unordered, mutable collection of unique elements  dictionary: mutable mappings of keys to values, insertion-ordered in the current implementation

Data structures provide different ways to organise and access Python objects, forming the backbone of most algorithms used in Finance.

Data Structures: Lists  Lists

A list is an ordered collection of Python objects.

Lists have the following key properties: Ordered:  Objects appear in a fixed order  This order is set by the programmer and not determined by the values of the objects. Mutable:  Objects may be added to or removed from the list.  Objects in the list may be replaced.

Elements in the list may be any Python object, and lists can contain multiple types of objects.

Data Structures: Lists - Construction

Lists are typically constructed using literals of the form: [ expression1, expression2, ...]

An opening square bracket [ Zero or more expressions, separated by commas A closing square bracket ]

1 # A list of the first three integers 2 first_three = [1, 2, 3] 4 # An empty list 5 empty_list = []

Data Structures: Lists - Construction

You are free to mix types within the list: 1 lst = [1, "string", True, None] 2 print(type(lst)) 3 # <class 'list'>

Lists may contain any objects, even other lists: 1 lst_a = [1, "string", True, None] 2 lst_b = ["a" , lst_a] 3 print(lst_b) 4 # ['a', [1, 'string', True, None]]

Data Structures: Lists - Indexing

We use square brackets to indicate that we want to retrieve an object from a list.

Python uses zero-based indexing: the initial object is at index 0. Each subsequent object in a list is indexed by the next relevant integer. Indexing beyond the last object of a list will result in an IndexError.

1 example = [1, "string", True, None] 2 example[0] # 1 3 example[1] # "string" 4 example[2] # True 5 example[3] # None 6 example[4] # IndexError

Data Structures: Lists - Indexing

You may also index the list “backwards” from the last object to the first using negative numbers.

The last object of the list is given index -1. Each previous object is indexed by the next negative integer. Negative indexing beyond the first object of a list will result in an IndexError. 1 example[-1] # None 2 example[-2] # True 3 example[-3] # "string" 4 example[-4] # 1 5 example[-5] # IndexError

Data Structures: Lists - Slices

Slices are used to access subsets of the list. They are defined using the following syntax:

The name of the source list An initial square bracket [ The index of the start object in the list (“start index”) The index of the stop object in the list (“stop index”) A closing square bracket ]

1 six_letters = ["a", "b", "c", "d", "e", "f"] 2 six_letters[1:4] # ["b", "c", "d"]

Slices include the object at the start index, but do not include the object at the stop index. Negative indices may be used.

Data Structures: Lists - Slices

One needs to be careful when slicing lists:

This decision to take a slice up to, but not including, the final index is counter-intuitive to new programmers (and some experienced programmers as well) Remember that the length of the slice is the difference between the start and end indices. The starting object must occur before the ending object in the list.

1 six_letters = ["a", "b", "c", "d", "e", "f"] 3 six_letters[-5:-2]) # ['b', 'c', 'd'] 4 six_letters[1:-2]) # ['b', 'c', 'd'] 5 six_letters[4:1]) # []

Data Structures: Lists - Slices

Python has shorthand for slicing lists from the beginning or to the end:

A slice from that starts at the first element may omit the first index: lst[:4] is equivalent to lst[0:4] Slicing up to and including the last element is achieved by omitting the last index: lst[1:] begins at the element at index 1 and runs through and including the last element. Unlike indexing, slices using endpoint locations before the first element of the list or beyond the end of a list do not raise errors.

1 six_letters = ["a", "b", "c", "d", "e", "f"] 3 six_letters[:-2] # ['a', 'b', 'c', 'd'] 4 six_letters[:0] # [] 5 six_letters[3:] # ['d', 'e', 'f'] 6 six_letters[3:100] # ['d', 'e', 'f'] 7 six_letters[-6:100] # ['a', 'b', 'c', 'd', 'e', 'f']

Data Structures: Lists - Reassigning objects

The object referenced at any location in a list may be reassigned by placing the list and index location on the left-hand side of an assignment statement:

Example: 1 lst = ["a", "b", "c"] 2 lst[1] = True 3 # lst is now ["a", True, "c"]

Data Structures: Lists Methods - Introduction

Lists are instances of the list class, which provides many useful methods

List methods differ in how they act on the list instance Methods that modify the list in place  Change the instance itself  May or may not produce any further information about the list  Methods that do not produce any further information will return None Methods that do not modify the list in place  Do not change the instance  Typically, return list elements or some information about the list

Understanding these categories helps you predict method behaviour and avoid common mistakes.

Data Structures: Lists – Methods that Modify in Place

Many list methods modify the list instance in place

In this lecture we will cover the .append method lst.append(x) -> None Add an object to the end of the list (modifies in place, returns None)

Example 1 lst = [1, 2] 2 lst.append(3) 3 print(lst) # [1, 2, 3]

The .append method returns None, so it does not produce any information about the list

Data Structures: Lists – Methods that Modify in Place

Other methods that modify lists in place include:

lst.extend(iterable) -> None Add all elements from another iterable (e.g. list, tuple, set) to the end. lst.sort() -> None Sort the list in place (ascending order by default). lst.insert(i, obj) -> None Insert an object at position i, shifting later elements to the right. lst.pop() -> object Remove and return the last element of the list. lst.pop(i) -> object Remove and return the element at position i. lst.remove(x) -> None Remove the first occurrence of value x from the list. lst.reverse() -> None Reverse the order of the list in place.

These methods will be discussed in class

Data Structures: Lists – Methods that do not modify in place

Some methods do not modify the list in place

Examples lst.count(x) -> int Return the number of times x appears in the list (does not modify)

1 lst = [1, 2, 2, 3] 2 print(lst.count(2)) # 2 3 print(lst) # [1, 2, 2, 3] (unchanged)

lst.index(x) -> int Return the index of the first occurrence of x in the list (does not modify). Raises a ValueError if x is not found.

1 fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry", "banana"] 3 print(fruits.index("banana")) # 1 4 print(fruits) # ["apple", "banana", "cherry", "banana"] 5 fruits.index("lemon") # ValueError

Data Structures: Lists - Commonly used methods

Python has a rich collection of list methods. While not critical at this point, you may wish to review the official documentation (or use AI) to see what else is available.

Data Structures: Tuples  Data Structures: Tuples

A tuple is an ordered collection of Python objects.

Tuples have the following key properties: Ordered:  Objects appear in a fixed order  This order is set by the programmer and not determined by the values of the objects. Immutable  A tuple has a fixed size when it is created, unlike a list, which can change in size.  Objects may not be added or removed from the list.  Objects in the list may not be replaced.

Elements may be any Python object, and tuples can contain multiple types of objects.

Data Structures: Tuples - Construction

Tuples are typically constructed using literals of the form: (object1, object2)

An opening parenthesis ( Zero or more objects, separated by commas A closing parenthesis )

1 # A three-object tuple 2 three_tuple = ("word", 5, False) 4 # An empty tuple (Both forms are valid) 5 empty_tuple = () 6 empty_tuple = tuple() 8 # A single object tuple requires a comma after the object 9 single_tuple = ("word",)

Data Structures: Tuples - Indexing

Indexing single objects of a tuple is supported using the same notation as for lists

Square bracket zero-based indexing. Backward (negative) indexing is supported. It is an IndexError to use an index outside the range.

1 t = ("word", 5, False) 3 # Accessing using indexing 4 t[0] # Also t[-3] 5 # "word" 6 t[1] # Also t[-2] 7 # 5 8 t[2] # Also t[-1] 9 # False 11 # Attempting to access an element 12 t[5] 13 # IndexError: tuple index out of range

Data Structures: Tuples - Slicing

Tuple slices are supported using the same notation as for lists

Square bracket zero-based indexing. Backward (negative) indexing is supported. It is an IndexError to use an index outside the range. 1 t = ("word", 5, False) 2 t[0:2] 3 # ("word", 5)

Data Structures: Tuples - Huh?. . . and also why?

One may wonder why tuples exist:

Tuples function similarly to lists. . . Ordered collections of Python objects Identical indexing and slicing syntax

. . . but seem less powerful. Tuples are immutable (can not be changed) Tuples may seem weaker than lists, but immutability has advantages:

Predictability: Contents cannot be altered accidentally Hashability: Tuples can be used as keys in dictionaries or elements in sets (discussed later) Efficiency: often lighter and slightly faster than lists

Data Structures: List and Tuple Constructors

Lists and tuples can also be created using their class constructors

1 # Constructing from an iterable 2 list("ABC") # ['A', 'B', 'C'] 3 tuple([1, 2, 3]) # (1, 2, 3) 5 # Empty containers 6 list() # [] 7 tuple() # ()

Constructors are especially useful when converting between types or from generators into concrete containers.

Data Structures: Lists and Tuples - Key Takeaways

Lists and tuples are both ordered collections of Python objects

Lists  Mutable: can grow, shrink, or change contents  Workhorse for storing data that evolves (e.g. time series, datasets) Tuples  Immutable: fixed once created  Useful when immutability is required

Both support indexing and slicing Both can hold any type of Python object, even other containers Lists and tuples can be created using literals ([...], (...)) or constructors (list(), tuple())
